paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## LEARNING MULTIPLE DATA DISTRIBUTIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Vulnerability to Distribution Shift is a major challenge for real-world applications of Deep Learning. Distribution Shift occurs when the test data distribution is not identical to the training distribution. We present Multi-Distribution Learning, exploring the effectiveness of Data Augmentation to prepare for Distribution Shift. We use common Data Augmentations to simulate multiple data distributions and test how many of these distributions can be learned with a single model. We first illustrate the impact of adding the test distribution to the training distribution, such that it is no longer a Distribution Shift. We find success with pairs of distributions, however, the model is limited when attempting to learn 10 distributions simultaneously. In order to search for a subset of distributions that train well together, we use a Lookahead search derived from research in Multi-Task Learning. From this analysis, we select 4 distributions with high affinities to train together and 1 negative distribution to use for performance comparison. These experiments confirm that the accuracy of models evaluated on a test set included in the training distribution is much higher compared to test distributions not included in training. We evaluate our distribution grouping with Zero-Shot Distribution Inference to a held-out positive distribution and the held-out negative distribution. We compare this Zero-Shot Distribution Inference to a strong baseline of the RandAugment training scheme, as well as to a weak baseline of a model trained without Data Augmentation. We conclude by discussing directions for future work in using Data Augmentation to combat Distribution Shift.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Distribution Shift, defined as the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set, are the Achilles heel of Deep Learning. We propose a simple solution to use Data Augmentation for targeted Generalization, alleviating the problem of Distribution Shift. In training, we aim to cover as many data distributions as possible, such that a Distribution Shift is not completely novel to our model. We measure our models using the Average Distribution Accuracy and propose a new framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics. These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.

We begin with two important questions for thinking about Distribution Shift. What are different data distributions? What does Distribution Shift in the real-world look like? A data distribution can be characterized based on the frequency of class labels. For example, the training set may contain 80% dogs and 20% cats, but the test distribution shifts to 40% dogs and 60% cats. In addition to distributions of labels, we can think about distributions of the inputs themselves. This is significantly harder to characterize due to the high-dimensional nature of raw inputs, such as pixel grids or word embeddings, or even intermediate vector representations from these sequential processing models.

The WILDS benchmark Koh et al. (2020) has been a very useful dataset to understand Distribution Shift in the real-world. Koh et al. collected examples of Distribution Shifts across applications in Wildlife Monitoring, Molecular Engineering, and Python Code Completion, to give a few examples. The authors characterize Distribution Shift into two categories, Subpopulation Shift and Domain Generalization. Subpopulation Shift is well illustrated by our previous example of a shifting frequency of cats and dogs. However, Subpopulation Shift can also refer to more high-level distributions, such as an increase in certain features of the input. Domain Generalization describes settings where the train and test domains are completely disjoint. For example, a sentiment classifier

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 1: The Multi-Distribution Learning framework evaluates how training with a data distribution helps with test performance on that distribution. The framework tests the limits of how many distributions we can learn with a single model.
trained on movie reviews and then tested on restaurant reviews Gururangan et al. (2020), or an image classifier trained on photorealistic images and tested on paintings Peng et al. (2019).

Another technique for studying Distribution Shift is through corruption tests. Datasets such as ImageNet-C Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019), ImageNet-Corrupted, are constructed by applying common Data Augmentations to the original ImageNet test set. Models that achieve high performance on ImageNet typically do not generalize to ImageNet-C as well. There are several other examples of these datasets, such as smaller-scale CIFAR-C and MNIST-C tests Mu & Gilmer (2019), ImageNet-P with more subtle corruptions, and Stylized-ImageNet Geirhos et al. (2019) which uses a style transfer algorithm to disentangle content from style in images. Corruption testing, achieved by applying Data Augmentation to an original test set, is an important lens for viewing the phenomenon of Distribution Shift. We explore how we can similarly use Data Augmentation to prepare for Distribution Shift and the limits of this technique.

Our objective is to use Data Augmentation to target the Generalization we want the model to achieve. This is a simple, but promising strategy to inject prior knowledge about how the distribution will shift. We believe these assumptions on the test data distribution are necessary to make progress, inspired by the following quote from Arjovsky (2019), “If the test data is arbitrary or unrelated to the training data, then generalization is obviously futile.” This quote supports the idea that we should make assumptions and bake in prior knowledge about the test distributions.

We begin our experiments by confirming that models which achieve high performance on an original test set will fail to generalize to the same test set corrupted with common data augmentations. This holds even for models trained with RandAugment Cubuk et al. (2020), a strong regularization strategy based on Data Augmentation. Our training strategy to achieve generalization to these test sets is illustrated in Figure 1. We apply N corruptions to the original data to form N training distributions. These N corruptions are additionally applied to the original test data to form an equivalent N test distributions. We report the average accuracy across the N test distributions. We begin by showing how this strategy increases the performance using N=2 augmentations.

We then scale our strategy up to learning N=10 distributions at once. We find that this requires much longer training times to achieve rivaling performance to single augmentation specializations trained with N=1. We borrow inspiration from research in Multi-Task Learning and use a Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for distributions that train well together. We refer to these distributions as having a high affinity with each other. We use a Lookahead search to compare the performance of a proposed gradient update after training on a given distribution. Distributions are determined to train well together if the proposed update improves the performance across both test sets. We further attempt to disentangle the impact of repeating data in our distribution construction strategy by partitioning the CIFAR-10 set into 5,000 images each, to remove the confounding factor of repeating data. We find similar distribution groupings with and without repeating the original data.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

We select 4 augmentations from our Lookahead search to train together. We additionally select 1 augmentation with a negative affinity for the sake of comparison. We find that the grouping achieves a much higher Average Distribution Score than the N=10 model trained with similar computation. We then generalize these experiments to a more realistic evaluation of Distribution Shift. We pose this as a problem of Zero-Shot Distribution Inference. We propose that groupings with a high affinity to the novel test distribution will perform better. We use a cross-validation analysis to iteratively pair 3 out of the 4 augmented distributions for training, evaluating the held-out distribution Zero-Shot. We find much better performance with this cluster compared to a strong RandAugment baseline, which is not completely Zero-Shot, as well as a weak baseline of a model trained without any Data Augmentation.

Our experiments utilize Data Augmentation to simulate diverse data distributions. We are currently limited to geometric transformations such as Rotations or Horizontal Flipping. However, we note that new frontiers in Data Augmentation such as CycleGAN Zhu et al. (2017) translations between domains or Text-to-Image generation Ramesh et al. (2021) should have a massive impact on this framework. We believe that Distribution Shift should be viewed similarly to Zero-Shot task inference commonly used in probing large models Liu et al. (2021). An interesting characteristic of Zero-Shot inference is the difference between massive models typically trained with less supervision than smaller, expert models. We view training on specialized distributions as analogous to supervised learning with particular tasks. Finally, we propose directions to extend our Distribution Generalization Score. Rather than the naive average accuracy, which we have reported in these experiments, we want a metric that rewards covering many distributions and the diversity of these distributions. We propose some ideas for measuring the diversity of these distributions.
In summary, we propose a framework of using Data Augmentation to anticipate and alleviate the challenges of Distribution Shift. Our contributions are as follows:

- We propose a new framework for Learning and Evaluating Multiple Data Distributions.

- We present a new metric for Distributional Generalization, the Average Distribution Score.
- We adapt a Task Grouping algorithm from Multi-Task Learning to study Multiple Distribution Learning.
- We present an analysis of Zero-Shot Distribution Inference, inspired by research on ZeroShot Task Inference.

## 2 RELATED WORK

## 2.1 DISTRIBUTION SHIFT

Understanding the nature of Generalization is one of the core goals of Artificial Intelligence Chollet (2019). Many recent works have characterized types of Generalization. These include Domain Transfer Peng et al. (2019), Compositional and Systematic Generalization Johnson et al. (2016), Concept Drift, or Robustness, to give a few examples. Further characterizations include In-Distribution versus Out-of-Distribution Generalization, viewing Generalization gaps in a similar lens as fine- and coarse-grained classification. From a practical perspective, Koh et al. (2020) have published the WILDS benchmark characterizing the generalization needed for many real-world applications. In this work, we propose a simple framework for studying the relationship between training and test distributions. Andreassen et al. (2021) and Wortsman et al. (2021) have also explored the relationship between Zero-Shot robustness and fine-tuned models. Regardless of the particular type of generalization, we can likely form Data Augmentations that communicate expected Distribution Shifts.

## 2.2 CORRUPTION TESTING

Many works have found that Deep Learning systems are vulnerable to corruption tests. These tests are generally head-scratching cases because they very closely resemble the original input. This defies a natural intuition about the data and requires thinking closely about the distributions of inputs. Corruption tests range from injecting adversarial noise maps to applying Data Augmentations, as we have done in this work. Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019) presented ImageNet-C, a dataset constructed

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

from a set of 75 Data Augmentations and ImageNet-P constructed from adversarial injections. Ovadia et al. (2019) used corruption sets to evaluate Uncertainty techniques in Deep Learning. **?** utilized style randomization to understand intrinsic properties of Convolutional Neural Networks, finding a texture bias.

## 2.3 DATA AUGMENTATION

Multi-Distribution Learning is a technique building on Data Augmentation for improving the Generalization of Deep Neural Networks. Many works have covered different Data Augmentation techniques across many domains. Some examples include Kernel Filters, Geometric Transformations, Random Erasing, Color Space Transformations, Mixing Images, Adversarial Training, Neural Style Transfer, and GAN Data Augmentation Shorten & Khoshgoftaar (2019). In addition to individual augmentations, recent works have explored new strategies for utilizing Data Augmentation. This includes application in contrastive losses in self-supervised learning such as SimCLR Chen et al. (2020), as well as consistency losses in Unsupervised Data Augmentation Xie et al. (2020). Sinha et al. (2021) have additionally explored utilizing Data Augmentations that intentionally create Out-of-Distribution data. Our work is similar in that we are looking for new ways to utilize Data Augmentation in Deep Learning.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

Our experiments report training and evaluation strategies for the ResNet152V2 He et al. (2016) on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiments are implemented using the Keras framework. The experiments are run on an NVIDIA A100 GPU in a Google Colab Pro+ runtime, with a maximum training time of roughly 20 hours. We experiment with up to 10 common Data Augmentations used for image data: Rotation, Translation, Crop, Cutout, Bright, Dark, Gamma Contrast, Horizontal Flip, Vertical Flip, and Jigsaw. These augmentations are implemented using the imgaug open-source library. Following is a quick description of how these augmentations transform the original data distribution.

The Rotation augmentation rotates an image along polar coordinates between 0 to 360 degrees. The Translation augmentation shifts an image along the x and y axes. This is done by moving pixels over/up by x pixels and adding 0s to displaced values. The Crop augmentation selects a subset of the original image and then resizes the subset to the original resolution with bilinear interpolation. The Cutout augmentation selects patches of the image and replaces the patch with 0 values. The Bright augmentation adds k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 255 (white). The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 0 (black). The Gamma Contrast augmentation alters the distribution of pixel values to a smaller window between 0 and 255. The Horizontal Flip (LR Flip) augmentation mirrors the original image along the y-axis. The Vertical Flip (UD Flip) augmentation inversely mirrors the original image along the x-axis. The Jigsaw augmentation divides the image into n pixel windows and shuffles their location in the image.

As a quick summary of our experiments, Training with 2 Distributions characterizes the problem of Distribution Shift and the simple solution of adding the test distribution to the training set. The model is not training with the test set itself, just the underlying distribution that will be encountered in-the-wild. We simulate the different distributions through Data Augmentations. We then scale this up to N=10 distributions in training. Lookahead Search to Group Training Distributions utilizes Lookahead analysis to find distributions that complement each other during training. Results of Grouping Training Distributions compares the Average Distribution Score with the grouped distributions to N=1 and N=10 distribution training strategies. Table 4 illustrates the Zero-Shot Distribution Inference of the grouping with a strong RandAugment baseline and a weak baseline of training with No Data Augmentation.

## 3.1 TRAINING WITH 2 DISTRIBUTIONS

We begin by highlighting the problem of Distribution Shift, reproducing it in our experimental setup, and conducting simple experiments within the framework described in Figure 1. As shown in Table 1, when trained without any Data Augmentation the model performs much worse on the Rotation

Table 1: The efficacy of training with a specialized distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy achieved on each data distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximumm accuracy for each data distribution.
Table 2: Learning curves for Multi-Distribution Learning of 10 Augmentations. The schematic of this training strategy is shown in Figure 1.

and Jigsaw distributions compared to the original CIFAR-10 data. Even though the RandAugment training scheme dramatically improves performance on the original set, it is still susceptible to these augmentation derived distribution shifts. We then report the efficacy of specialized training on the distribution used in testing, such that it is no longer a Distribution Shift. We shift our aim to learning to specialize on multiple distributions in training. We consider two high-level schema for doing this. The first of which is Sequential Training (abbreviated as Seq in Table 1). Sequential Training refers to first learning the Rotation distribution and then learning the Jigsaw distribution. We encounter an interesting case of Catastrophic Forgetting where performance on the previously learned distribution quickly drops as it shifts focus to another data distribution. We find a better result with Alternating Training (abbreviated as Alt in Table 1). In Alternating Training we switch between each training distribution every epoch, rather than after convergence. We additionally test sharing a base feature extractor between each distribution and using separate classification heads to model each distribution (abbreviated as Alt-SH in Table 1). We do not find much difference with separate heads compared to fine-tuning the entire model. We continue with Alternating Training throughout the rest of the experiments.

## 3.2 TRAINING WITH 10 DISTRIBUTIONS

Following the success of learning 2 distributions in training, we set our sights on increasing the number of learned data distributions. Table 2 illustrates the learning curve of 10 data distributions. We find that it takes much longer for this strategy to achieve a strong Average Augmentation Score. However, after 1,000 epochs (roughly 20 hours of training with our computing setup described previously), the model achieves a fairly strong Average Augmentation Score with small variance and no significant minimum or maximum outliers in the accuracy scores. Throughout training the Rotation, Translation, and Crop Augmentations have the lowest performance, whereas Vertical and Horizontal Flipping (shown as UD Flip and LR Flip) have the highest score. At the end of training the multi-distribution learning model achieves a nearly identical score on the Horizontal Flip as the Original Test set, it could be interesting future work to see if the model has achieved invariance to this distribution shift in the prediction and representation spaces. We find more interesting details about the Vertical Flip augmentation in our analysis of Lookahead Search.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 2: This table shows the performance difference by taking the proposed gradient update on the y-axis on the test distributions on the x-axis. In this index: Rotate = 0, Translate = 1, Crop = 2, Cutout = 3, Bright = 4, Dark = 5, Gamma Contrast = 6, LR Flip = 7, UD Flip = 8, and Jigsaw = 9.

## 3.3 LOOKAHEAD SEARCH TO GROUP TRAINING DISTRIBUTIONS

Due to the long training time of learning 10 distributions at once, we aim to find a subset of distributions that have a faster learning curve and higher performance. We adapt the Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for this subset. This algorithm uses a Lookahead search to compute what the change in performance on other tasks, or distributions in our example, will change. For example, in Multi-Task Learning the Task Groupings algorithm computes the change in performance on Semantic Segmentation after taking a gradient update on Object Detection. We similarly compute the change in performance on the Translation distribution after taking a gradient step on the Rotated training set.

The results of the Distribution Grouping analysis are shown in Figure 2. The x-axis is the evaluated distribution and the y-axis is the distribution that proposes a gradient update. The augmentations are indexed according to their position in the following list: Rotate = 0, Translate = 1, Crop = 2, Cutout = 3, Bright = 4, Dark = 5, Gamma Contrast = 6, LR Flip = 7, UD Flip = 8, and Jigsaw = 9. We find a strong outlier result where a proposed gradient update on Vertical Flipping (UD Flip) causes a significant drop in performance across the other augmentations. This likely explains the optimization difficulty behind learning all 10 distributions simultaneously. The second strongest negative result is the impact of updating on Bright and evaluating on the Dark test distribution. We leave it to future work to explore if the inverse augmentations of increased and decreased Brightness have opposite gradient directions.

From Figure 2, we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw to form our subset. In order to further understand the impact of a gradient update on one distribution to the performance of another, we avoid repeating data. For example, the rotation distribution still has roughly the same image as the translation distribution in Figure 2. The right hand side of Figure 3 illustrates the lookahead updates when we split the data such that no distribution has the same original CIFAR-10 image. We find a similar grouping analysis with and without repeating data.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 3: A Side-by-Side view of the impact of repeating data for Lookahead update analysis. Please see the caption of Figure 2 for more details on the x and y axes and the distribution indexing from 0 to 9.

## 3.4 RESULTS OF GROUPING TRAINING DISTRIBUTIONS

We analyze the performance of our distribution grouping on learning the training distributions and zero-shot inference to novel distributions. Table 3 reports enumerating through 3 of the 4 selected distributions for training. We encountered an optimization difficulty with the Rotate, Translate, Jigsaw (abbreviated as RTJ in Table3) that achieves low performance. However, the other 3 clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation scheme other than the Jigsaw test. We find an interesting division between distributions that benefit from specialization (Rotation and Translation) and others that benefit from the ensemble (Darker and Jigsaw). This could be an interesting property for the design of ensembles and specialist distribution models that we leave for future work.

The ultimate goal of learning multiple data distributions is to lessen the decrease in accuracy when faced with Distribution Shift. We test how the grouped distributions help with Zero-Shot Distribution Inference through a cross-validation analysis within the group. For example, we hold-out the Rotation distribution and train with Translate, Darker, and Jigsaw. The entires for 3 Augmentations in Table 4 reflect the Zero-Shot accuracy without training on that augmented distribution. For Rotation and Translation, we find a very strong Zero-Shot generalization with the group. As mentioned in Table 3, we ran into optimization difficulties with our run leaving out the Darker distribution and it achieves a very low accuracy. We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization. We held-out Gamma Contrast as an example of a distribution with a negative affinity to the group. We report the best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast, supporting our analysis that the negative augmentation does not benefit from this particular cluster of training distributions. We compare this with the RandAugment model reported in Table 1, which achieves 83.8% accuracy on the original CIFAR-10 test set. Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot. The No Augmentation baseline highlights the poor performance when faced with Distribution Shift. In both RandAugment and No Augmentation we see the lowest performance decrease with the weakest augmentation, Darker. This highlights the need to develop distribution distance scores to weight the performances.

## 4 DISCUSSION

## 4.1 FUTURE DIRECTIONS FOR SIMULATING DATA DISTRIBUTIONS

Our experiments tested simple data augmentations to simulate distribution shifts. We have used the terms “augmentations” and “distributions” interchangeably to propose their similarities. We

Table 3: Comparison of Generalization to Test Distributions included in the Training Distribution.

|TrainingDistributions|Rotate Test|Translate Test|Darker Test|Jigsaw Test|Gamma Contrast Test|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|3 Augmentations|**72.4%**|**71.9%**|45.5%|65.2%|62.6%|
|RandAugment|57.4%|49.7%|**82.2%**|**69.5%**|**72.6%**|
|No Augmentation|46.5%|35.6%|68.1%|62.5%|54.2%|

Table 4: Zero-Shot Distribution Inference.

believe techniques such as CycleGAN Zhu et al. (2017) and DALL-E Ramesh et al. (2021) will allow us to control Domain Generalization with Data Augmentation-style interfaces. For example, the iWildlife-monitoring camera in the WILDS benchmark encounters a new location for a wildelife monitoring camera at test time. A CycleGAN may be able to map the existing training data into this new location, further guided with text interfaces from DALL-E. We propose that we can train with this inferred novel distribution to perform better in the Distribution Shift.

## 4.2 ZERO-SHOT DISTRIBUTION INFERENCE

Few-Shot Learning describes learning a new task with a few examples, hopefully achieved by leveraging information from previously learned tasks. Zero-Shot Learning refers to a more extreme case in which the model performs a new task, but no additional learning is allowed. We can similarly view Distribution Shifts as having a few examples from the new distribution or having to perform Zero-Shot task inference. Another interesting trend in Few- and Zero-Shot learning is the efficacy of larger-scale models. Models such as GPT-3 can be guided with prompts to perform novel language tasks. It remains to be seen whether collections of expert models assigned to their respective inferences will outperform a single monolithic model. Even if it becomes unnecessary to train expert models for collections of tasks, or distributions as we have argued, understanding novel tasks will still be useful for evaluation.

## 4.3 DISTRIBUTION GENERALIZATION SCORE

Our experiments report a simple average augmentation accuracy. For future work we intend to explore metrics that better capture the diversity of a model’s generalization ability. We intend to weight the average accuracies by the number of evaluated distributions and the distance between these distributions. The distance between distributions is a challenging quantity to measure. One solution, similar to ideas in the automated evaluation of generated images such as the Inception Score, could be to embed each distribution into the feature space of a pre-trained classifier and use these vector representations to compute distances. A strong Distribution Generalization Score should also be correlated with performance on Zero-Shot Distribution Inference.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In Conclusion, we have presented Multi-Distribution Learning, a framework to study the impact of training on potential test distributions. This is a simple framework to leverage the prior knowledge about how a data distribution is likely to change in deployment. Key to our framework are the number of distributions used in training and the metric used to score the diversity of distribution performance. We have presented several experiments that highlight the challenge of learning many distributions at once. We have additionally presented a technique to group together distributions for

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

training. Finally, we propose framing the problem of Distribution Shift as analogous to Zero-Shot Task Inference. We believe that rapid advances in Data Augmentation and Generative Modeling will have a large impact on this research direction.

## REFERENCES

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an empirical exploration of 'Multi-Distribution Learning' using data augmentations to simulate and mitigate distribution shifts. While the core idea of using Task Grouping algorithms from Multi-Task Learning (MTL) to identify compatible augmentations is interesting and well-motivated, the execution suffers from several technical inconsistencies. Specifically, the data reported in the tables occasionally contradicts the text, some key performance metrics for baselines are missing, and the 'Zero-Shot' claims are somewhat overstated given the overlap between RandAugment and the proposed training sets. The paper demonstrates a good grasp of the WILDS benchmark and MTL literature but lacks the rigor in numerical reporting required for a top-tier conference.

comments.title: Inconsistency between text and Table 1 bolding
comments.quote: The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy achieved on each data distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximumm accuracy for each data distribution.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 1, the 'Original Test' column has 83.8% bolded (RandAugment), which is correct. However, for 'Jigsaw Test', the value 75.0% (Alt-SH-RJ) is bolded, even though the text later suggests 1000-epoch training (Table 3) reaches 76.0%. More importantly, the 'Average Distribution Score' column has 74.3% (Alt-RJ) bolded as the maximum, but Alt-SH-RJ is 74.2%. While the bolding is mostly correct within the context of Table 1, the double-sentence caption and minor discrepancy in selection (bolding 75.0% instead of emphasizing which specific strategy won) is slightly confusing. More critically, the text claims Alt-RJ is the best, but the 'Avg Distribution Score' is just a mean of the three previous columns, and the math for Alt-RJ (80.0, 73.7, 74.8) averages to 76.16%, not 74.3%.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in Average Distribution Score
comments.explanation: I attempted to verify the 'Average Distribution Score' for the 'Alt-RJ' row in Table 1. The average of 80.0, 73.3, and 74.8 is actually 76.16%. The reported value of 74.3% does not match the arithmetic mean of the listed components. This happens in several rows (e.g., No Augmentation: (73.9+41.6+61.3)/3 = 58.9%, not 51.5%). It appears the 'Average Distribution Score' might be calculated over a larger set of augmentations not shown in the table, or there is a derivation error. This is a significant issue as it is the paper's primary metric.

comments.title: Contradiction regarding training time impact
comments.quote: We find that this requires much longer training times to achieve rivaling performance to single augmentation specializations trained with N=1.
comments.explanation: While the authors claim N=10 requires much longer training, Table 3 shows that '10 Augs, 1000 Epochs' achieves 70.9% on Rotate, while the '1 Aug' specialist achieves 75.2%. However, for Jigsaw, the '10 Augs' model (76.0%) actually outperforms the '1 Aug' specialist (71.6%). This contradicts the general narrative that the N=10 model is 'limited' or always inferior to specialists; in the case of Jigsaw, the multi-distribution training actually provided a benefit over specialization. This nuance is missing from the prose and makes the 'Task Grouping' motivation feel less urgent for certain augmentations.

comments.title: Ambiguous 'Zero-Shot' definition for RandAugment
comments.quote: We compare this Zero-Shot Distribution Inference to a strong baseline of the RandAugment training scheme... it has seen compositions of them... Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that RandAugment isn't 'truly zero-shot' because it includes the transformations in its policy. However, in Table 4, RandAugment outperforms the proposed 'Zero-Shot' 3-Augmentation grouping on 3 out of 5 tests (Darker, Jigsaw, Gamma Contrast). If the 3-Aug model is intended to demonstrate the power of grouping for out-of-distribution generalization, the fact that a standard (non-specialized) baseline like RandAugment wins most categories undermines the necessity of the proposed Lookahead search for 'Zero-Shot' purposes. The claim of 'much better performance' in the abstract is not supported by Table 4.

comments.title: Missing justification for Task Grouping selection
comments.quote: From Figure 2, we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw to form our subset.
comments.explanation: The authors select these four based on Figure 2 (affinities). However, Figure 2 shows that Rotation (0) has a negative impact on almost everything except itself. Specifically, the row for index 0 (Rotate) has values that look negative or neutral. Meanwhile, Bright (4) and Dark (5) show strong positive affinities for each other. The logic for choosing Jigsaw (9) and Rotation (0) together, despite Rotation's seemingly low affinity scores in the heatmap, is not fully explained. A reader cannot reproduce the 'selection' criteria because 'high affinity' isn't mathematically defined in the text (e.g., as a threshold of the lookahead values).

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and timely investigation into multi-distribution learning using data augmentation to address distribution shift. The core ideas—leveraging augmentation to simulate test-time shifts, using lookahead-based grouping to identify synergistic distributions, and framing zero-shot distribution inference—are conceptually sound and well-motivated. The empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10 with ResNet152V2 is thorough, and the ablation studies provide useful insights into training dynamics and catastrophic forgetting. However, there are several technical ambiguities and inconsistencies, particularly around evaluation metrics, reproducibility of the grouping algorithm, and misalignment between claimed contributions and actual implementation. While the framework is promising, the lack of precise method specification and some overstatements in claims—especially regarding metric novelty and baseline comparisons—undermine the paper's impact and reproducibility.

comments.title: Misalignment between Average Distribution Score and table values
comments.quote:
|TrainingTechnique|Original Test|Rotation Test|Jigsaw Test|Average Distribution Score|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|No Augmentation|73.9%|41.6%|61.3%|51.5%|
comments.explanation: I initially assumed the Average Distribution Score was the arithmetic mean of the test accuracies across the three distributions reported in the row. However, (73.9 + 41.6 + 61.3)/3 ≈ 58.9%, not 51.5%. This suggests either a different weighting scheme or a different set of distributions being averaged. The paper states 'we report the average accuracy across the N test distributions', but here N=3. The discrepancy is not explained in the text or footnotes. Later, in Section 4.3, the authors mention wanting to weight by 'number of distributions and distance between these distributions', implying the current score isn’t just a mean. However, Table 1 presents it as if it's a standard average. This creates confusion: is the current Average Distribution Score already weighted, or is the 51.5% the result of a calculation error? No justification is given for the weights, nor is the formula provided. This undermines the interpretability of the core evaluation metric.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for 'Darker' vs 'Dark' augmentation
comments.quote: The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value in the image... |3 Augs(RDJ)|72.7%|N/A|81.0%|66.3%|73.3%|
comments.explanation: In the text describing augmentations, 'Dark' is used: 'The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value'. However, in Table 3 and Table 4, the column is labeled 'Darker Test', and in the text, the authors refer to 'Darker' (e.g., 'leaving out the Darker distribution'). The augmentation list does not include 'Darker' as a distinct method. This suggests either OCR corruption or author inconsistency. Given that 'Darker' appears consistently in results tables and discussion, while 'Dark' only in the method description, it is likely that 'Dark' was intended to mean 'Darker'. However, without clarification, a reader might assume 'Darker' is a different transformation (e.g., non-linear), especially since 'Bright' is also listed and could imply symmetry. This is a notation inconsistency that could confuse reproduction.

comments.title: Underspecified Lookahead Search implementation
comments.quote: We adapt the Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for this subset. This algorithm uses a Lookahead search to compute what the change in performance on other tasks... will change.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to adapt Fifty et al.'s Task Grouping algorithm, but the exact mechanism is underspecified. Specifically: (1) How frequently is the lookahead computed—every epoch, every batch, or only during initial analysis? (2) Is the gradient step simulated (e.g., via first-order approximation) or actually applied and rolled back? (3) How is the 'change in performance' quantified—mean accuracy change, relative improvement, or per-class metric? (4) What aggregation is used to determine 'high affinity' across multiple test distributions? The paper presents Figure 2 as the result of this search but offers no algorithmic pseudocode, hyperparameters (e.g., step size, batch size), or computational budget. Crucially, the method says 'we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw' based on Figure 2, but Figure 2 shows many negative interactions (e.g., UD Flip harming others); there’s no clear threshold or rule for which distributions are selected. Without these details, the method cannot be reproduced.

comments.title: Questionable claim about RandAugment baseline not being truly Zero-Shot
comments.quote: Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that RandAugment is not a valid zero-shot baseline because it applies compositions of augmentations (e.g., rotation and translation) to images, implying overlap with the test distributions. However, this misrepresents RandAugment: it applies a *stochastic mixture* of single or few augmentations per image, not deterministic compositions matching the exact test-time shifts. The paper provides no evidence that RandAugment’s policy covers the exact combinations used in evaluation (e.g., pure rotation without other transforms). In fact, standard RandAugment policies are designed for invariance to mild augmentations, not full distribution alignment. While it's valid to argue RandAugment has seen related perturbations, claiming it undermines 'truly Zero-Shot' sets an unrealistic standard—few real-world models are trained on completely disjoint data. This overstates the advantage of the proposed method and mischaracterizes a well-established baseline.

comments.title: Ambiguity in '10 Augmentations' training curve time alignment
comments.quote: Table 2: Learning curves for Multi-Distribution Learning of 10 Augmentations... |Epoch|10|30|50|200|350|1000|
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports results at Epochs 10, 30, ..., 1000 for the 10-distribution model, claiming 1000 epochs takes ~20 hours. Elsewhere, the N=2 and grouped models use Alternating Training. However, it is unclear whether 'epoch' means one pass through a single augmented dataset or all 10. If the former, then 1000 epochs per distribution would imply 10,000 full cycles—unlikely in 20 hours. If the latter, then 'epoch' refers to cycling through all 10, meaning each distribution sees 1/10th the data per epoch. This drastically affects the comparison to the N=1 models (e.g., 'Rotation' in Table 1), which likely use full epochs per distribution. The paper does not define what constitutes an epoch in the multi-distribution setting, risking misinterpretation of convergence speed and sample efficiency.

comments.title: Metric novelty overstated for Average Distribution Score
comments.quote: We present a new metric for Distributional Generalization, the Average Distribution Score.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the Average Distribution Score as a 'new metric'. However, it appears to be a standard arithmetic (or possibly weighted) mean of accuracies across multiple test sets—commonly used in domain generalization (e.g., in WILDS benchmarks). While the paper does suggest future enhancements (weighting by diversity), the metric as reported in Tables 1–4 is not novel. The claim overlooks prior work that already uses similar aggregation for multi-distribution evaluation. This is a framing issue: the *framework* of evaluating multiple augmented distributions is novel, but the core scoring mechanism is not. The contribution would be better stated as applying a known aggregation within a new context.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting framework for using data augmentation to prepare for distribution shift, with some promising empirical results. The core idea of training on multiple simulated distributions is sensible, and the adaptation of multi-task learning techniques for distribution grouping is innovative. However, the paper suffers from several methodological ambiguities and inconsistencies that undermine its claims. The most significant issue is the disconnect between the proposed 'Average Distribution Score' metric (which should account for distribution diversity) and the simple average actually used in experiments. Additionally, key methods like the Lookahead search and alternation training schedule are underspecified, making reproducibility difficult. While the empirical results show some potential, the lack of methodological rigor and inconsistent terminology weaken the paper's contributions.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of Average Distribution Score
comments.quote: We measure our models using the Average Distribution Accuracy and propose a new framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics. These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The paper defines the Average Distribution Score as a weighted average that should account for both the number of distributions and their diversity. However, throughout the experiments, the authors use a simple arithmetic mean of accuracies across distributions without any weighting for diversity or number of distributions. For example, Tables 1, 3, and 4 all show simple averages. This is a significant inconsistency between the proposed metric and its implementation, undermining the novelty of the claimed contribution.

comments.title: Underspecified Lookahead search method
comments.quote: We adapt the Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for this subset. This algorithm uses a Lookahead search to compute what the change in performance on other tasks, or distributions in our example, will change.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions adapting the Task Groupings algorithm but provides insufficient details for reproducibility. Key implementation details are missing: How many gradient steps are used for the lookahead? What batch sizes are used? How is the performance change measured (accuracy difference, relative improvement)? Without these details, readers cannot understand or reproduce the grouping analysis shown in Figures 2 and 3.

comments.title: Underspecified alternation training schedule
comments.quote: In Alternating Training we switch between each training distribution every epoch, rather than after convergence.
comments.explanation: The paper describes alternation training as switching between distributions every epoch, but leaves important details unspecified. Is the model trained on the full dataset of one distribution for one epoch, then switched? Or is the epoch split across distributions? How is the learning rate handled across switches? These details are crucial for understanding the optimization dynamics and for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous relationship between augmentations and distributions
comments.quote: We have used the terms 'augmentations' and 'distributions' interchangeably to propose their similarities.
comments.explanation: While the authors state they use these terms interchangeably, this creates confusion. Data augmentations are transformations applied to data, while distributions are statistical properties of data. The paper never clarifies whether each augmentation creates a distinct distribution or if augmentations are merely tools to simulate distributions. This ambiguity makes it difficult to interpret the results and their implications for real distribution shift.

comments.title: Questionable claim about RandAugment not being zero-shot
comments.quote: Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: The authors claim RandAugment is not truly zero-shot because it has seen compositions of augmentations. However, this argument is questionable. Zero-shot typically refers to not having seen the specific test distribution during training. Even if RandAugment has seen compositions, it has not seen the specific test distributions (e.g., pure rotation at test time) in isolation. This claim seems to unfairly dismiss a reasonable baseline.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for performance metric
comments.quote: Average AugScore (Table 2 header) vs Average Distribution Score (text)
comments.explanation: The paper uses 'Average Distribution Score' in the text and abstract, but Table 2 uses 'Average AugScore'. While this appears to refer to the same metric (simple average of accuracies), the inconsistent terminology creates confusion. This is particularly problematic given the earlier issue with the metric definition.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a clear and interesting framework for analyzing how various data augmentations affect model generalization to distribution shifts. While the experimental setup is well-motivated, there are notable inconsistencies in reporting the results, particularly between the text and the provided tables. Specifically, the paper struggles with internal consistency regarding data labeling and the validation of its central methodology, which makes it challenging to draw firm conclusions from the reported findings.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Table 1 caption and text
comments.quote: The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy achieved on each data distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximumm accuracy for each data distribution.
comments.explanation: The caption for Table 1 is redundant and contains a typo. More importantly, the table itself uses bolding in a way that is hard to justify. For example, in the 'Original Test' column, 83.8% is bolded, but in the 'Rotation Test' column, 75.2% is bolded, while 73.7% and 73.3% are not. The text states the bolded values indicate the maximum, but it is unclear if this is per-row or per-column, and some rows (like Alt-RJ) achieve higher scores than the bolded values in other rows of the same column. This ambiguity makes the table difficult to verify.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of Distribution Generalization Score
comments.quote: These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The prose provides a conceptual definition of the 'Distribution Generalization Score' (or 'Average Distribution Score') that involves multiple factors like diversity and number of distributions. However, in the experiments, this term is consistently used interchangeably with simple average accuracy. There is no formula or weighting provided that incorporates 'diversity'. As a reader, I cannot determine how the 'diversity' component is calculated, which is a major premise of the proposed framework.

comments.title: Missing justification for lookahead search mechanism
comments.quote: We use a Lookahead search to compare the performance of a proposed gradient update after training on a given distribution. Distributions are determined to train well together if the proposed update improves the performance across both test sets.
comments.explanation: The paper cites Fifty et al. (2021) for the Task Groupings algorithm but fails to describe the actual mechanism or objective function used for the 'Lookahead' step in this specific context. In particular, it is not explained how the gradient update on one distribution is projected or measured against the held-out test distribution. The leap from 'taking a gradient step' to 'improving performance' is non-trivial, and lacking the explicit formulation or procedure creates a barrier to reproducibility.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an intuitive and practically motivated framework for studying distribution shift via data augmentation grouping, with clear experimental setups on CIFAR-10. The adaptation of multi-task learning grouping heuristics to data augmentation selection is a solid conceptual contribution. However, the manuscript suffers from notable disconnects between claimed contributions and experimental execution, particularly regarding the proposed evaluation metric and the use of zero-shot terminology. Additionally, key methodological details regarding the lookahead procedure and augmentation naming are inconsistently described, which hinders reproducibility. With clarified definitions, precise methodological specifications, and more accurate framing of the evaluation protocols, this work could make a strong contribution to the distribution shift literature.

comments.title: Disconnect between claimed metric definition and experimental implementation
comments.quote: These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: I initially focused on the abstract and introduction, which explicitly define the proposed metric as a weighted average that accounts for both the number of distributions covered and their diversity. This sets a clear expectation for a novel, composite scoring function. However, upon checking Section 4.3, the authors state: 'Our experiments report a simple average augmentation accuracy. For future work we intend to explore metrics that better capture the diversity of a model’s generalization ability.' The context confirms that the weighted/diversity formulation is genuinely deferred to future work, meaning the 'new metric' presented is merely a standard mean accuracy. This remains problematic because it overstates the novelty of the contribution and leaves the reader wondering why the more complex definition was foregrounded in the introduction and contribution list when it was not actually implemented or evaluated.

comments.title: Underspecified lookahead search procedure for distribution grouping
comments.quote: We use a Lookahead search to compare the performance of a proposed gradient update after training on a given distribution. Distributions are determined to train well together if the proposed update improves the performance across both test sets.
comments.explanation: I was drawn to the authors' adaptation of the Task Groupings algorithm from Fifty et al. (2021) as a clever way to avoid the optimization conflicts seen when training all 10 distributions simultaneously. However, when I checked for methodological details, I found the procedure underspecified. The text does not clarify whether the 'proposed gradient update' consists of a single optimization step, multiple steps, or a specific learning rate. Crucially, it is not stated whether model parameters are reset to their pre-update state after computing the performance change on other distributions, or whether the update is retained. Furthermore, the mapping from the pairwise 10x16 matrix in Figure 2 to the final selection of four distributions is mathematically opaque. Without step size, parameter-reset, and aggregation logic, a reader cannot reproduce the grouping analysis. This is a moderate issue that directly impacts reproducibility.

comments.title: Misapplication of 'Zero-Shot Distribution Inference' terminology
comments.quote: We pose this as a problem of Zero-Shot Distribution Inference. We propose that groupings with a high affinity to the novel test distribution will perform better.
comments.explanation: The authors frame their cross-validation experiment—training on three augmented distributions and evaluating on the held-out fourth—as 'Zero-Shot Distribution Inference.' Initially, I assumed this implied a setup with no training examples for the target distribution or a feature-space/prompt-based transfer protocol common in zero-shot literature. Checking the context in Section 3.4 confirms the model is explicitly trained on three closely related distributions and simply evaluated on the fourth. This is standard transfer learning or domain generalization evaluation, not zero-shot inference. The authors later note that RandAugment is 'not truly Zero-Shot' because it sees augmentation compositions, which inadvertently highlights their loose use of the term. While the intuition behind testing out-of-distribution generalization is valid, labeling a standard train/test split as zero-shot could mislead readers expecting formal zero-shot protocols or evaluation metrics. This is a moderate conceptual framing issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming of the 'Dark' augmentation
comments.quote: The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value in the image...
comments.explanation: I initially noticed a shift in terminology when reading Section 3.4 and Tables 3 and 4, where the augmentation is repeatedly referred to as 'Darker' or 'Darker distribution', whereas Section 3 defines it as 'Dark'. After checking the surrounding context and the augmentation index, it is clear this refers to the exact same transformation. The inconsistency does not affect the technical validity or results, but it disrupts notation flow and could cause confusion for a careful reader verifying the table contents against the method description. This is a minor editorial issue that is easily resolved by standardizing the name throughout the manuscript.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper explores using data augmentation to train models on multiple simulated distributions to mitigate distribution shift. While the motivation is reasonable, the contributions are overstated. The proposed metric is inconsistently applied and the grouping algorithm is borrowed without sufficient detail for reproducibility. The experimental results show mixed performance, and comparisons to baselines are not adequately discussed. The paper needs clearer definitions, more rigorous evaluation, and better contextualization of results.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of Average Distribution Score
comments.quote: The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy achieved on each data distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximumm accuracy for each data distribution.
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the 'Average Distribution Score' is computed as the average of only the two augmented test sets (Rotation and Jigsaw), excluding the Original Test. However, in later tables (e.g., Table 3), the average includes all evaluated distributions that are part of the training set. The metric is not formally defined in the text, and its varying composition makes cross-table comparisons difficult. This could mislead readers into interpreting the metric as a consistent measure of generalization. Clarifying the metric's definition and applying it uniformly would resolve this.

comments.title: Underspecified adaptation of Lookahead search
comments.quote: We adapt the Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for this subset. This algorithm uses a Lookahead search to compute what the change in performance on other tasks, or distributions in our example, will change.
comments.explanation: The authors do not describe how the Lookahead search is implemented for their setting. For instance, what is the initial model state? How many gradient steps are taken? How is the performance change measured (accuracy, loss)? The paper does not specify which adaptation of Fifty et al. is used, nor does it provide algorithmic details. Without these, the procedure cannot be reproduced, which is a significant gap for a claimed contribution.

comments.title: Questionable claim about Zero-Shot Distribution Inference strength
comments.quote: We find a very strong Zero-Shot generalization with the group.
comments.explanation: In Table 4, when Darker is held out, the grouped model achieves only 45.5%, whereas RandAugment achieves 82.2% and even no augmentation achieves 68.1%. This contradicts the claimed strong generalization. The paper mentions 'optimization difficulties' but does not analyze why the grouping fails for Darker while succeeding for Rotation and Translation. Overclaiming the effectiveness without addressing such failures is a concern.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Table 4 reporting
comments.quote: 3 Augmentations
comments.explanation: The row labeled '3 Augmentations' gives single values for each test distribution (Rotate, Translate, Darker, Jigsaw, Gamma Contrast). It is unclear whether these values come from a single model or from cross-validation (each value from a different model that held out that distribution). The text mentions cross-validation but the table does not indicate which distributions were held out for each test. This ambiguity could lead a reader to misinterpret the results as coming from a single model.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel framework for Multi-Distribution Learning using data augmentations to simulate distribution shift. The experiments are well-structured and the insights regarding training groupings and lookahead search are valuable. However, the paper introduces a new metric called the 'Average Distribution Score' without a precise mathematical definition, leading to inconsistency between the reported scores and the calculated averages of the accuracy columns. Additionally, there are minor inconsistencies in variable naming and naming conventions within tables that require clarification.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of Average Distribution Score metric
comments.quote: These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The text defines the 'Average Distribution Score' in the Introduction using a weighted average of accuracy, the count of distributions, and diversity. However, in Table 1, the 'Average Distribution Score' for the 'No Augmentation' model is reported as 51.5%. The arithmetic mean of the three accuracy columns shown in the same table (73.9%, 41.6%, 61.3%) is approximately 58.9%. The discrepancy suggests that the metric includes non-standard weights or transformations for the 'number of distributions' and 'diversity' that are not quantified in the text. The proposed 'framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics' relies on this score, and without a reproducible formula or definition of the weighting scheme, the validity of the performance comparisons is undermined.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming of the 'Dark' augmentation
comments.quote: The Dark augmentation... In Figure 2, we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw... We held-out the Darker distribution...
comments.explanation: The paper defines the augmentation as 'Dark' in Section 3. However, throughout Section 3.4 and Tables 3 and 4, the same augmentation is referred to as 'Darker'. This inconsistency in variable naming makes it difficult to trace the specific augmentation or result corresponding to the 'Darker' column in the tables back to the definition provided in the Methods section.

comments.title: Overstatement of performance equivalence
comments.quote: At the end of training the multi-distribution learning model achieves a nearly identical score on the Horizontal Flip as the Original Test set...
comments.explanation: This claim is made in the context of the learning curves described by Table 2, which reports aggregate statistics (Average, Min, Max AugScore). While the 'Max AugScore' is 83.2%, the text asserts that the Horizontal Flip score is 'nearly identical' to the Original Test score (83.4%). The table does not explicitly list the specific accuracy for the Horizontal Flip distribution. Relying on the 'Max AugScore' to confirm a specific numeric equivalence is a potential overstatement.

comments.title: Inconsistent method abbreviation in Table 1
comments.quote: We additionally test sharing a base feature extractor between each distribution and using separate classification heads to model each distribution (abbreviated as Alt-SH in Table 1).
comments.explanation: The text explicitly abbreviates the experiment with separate heads as 'Alt-SH'. However, Table 1 lists the corresponding result row as 'Alt-SH-RJ'. The abbreviation 'Alt-SH' does not appear in Table 1, creating ambiguity about whether 'Alt-SH-RJ' is the full name of the model or if 'Alt-SH' refers to a distinct configuration not shown in the table.
